coredata.dc:title: Glycosylated liposomes loading carbon dots for targeted recognition to HepG2 cells
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Ce
Zhao, Yangyang
Hou, Yiting
Shan, Guiye
Yan, Dongmei
Liu, Yichun
coredata.dc:description: It is important to obtain the targeted nanocarriers during cancer treatment. Herein, mannosylated liposomes encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) are provided for targeted recognition of liver cancer HepG2 cells, which is based on the specific interaction between D-mannose and glycoprotein on the surface of HepG2 cells. CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. Then, they were encapsulated into liposomes by hydrophobic force. The encapsulation of CDs into liposomes increases their stability and fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, D-mannose can also be inserted into liposomes by the aldehyde amine reaction between aldehyde groups of mannose molecules and amino groups of liposomes. The obtained D-mannose-CDs-liposomes (Man-CDs-liposomes) exhibit selectively tracking and efficiently labelling for cancer cells. The work highlights the potential application of CDs for bioimaging and diagnostic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
H2O2
Specific binding
Targeted imaging
D-mannose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphology characterization of CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes by negative staining
The fluorescence yield enhancement of CDs by oxidation treatment of H2O2
The optical properties of liposomes loading CDs
The stability of liposomes loading with CDs
The cytotoxicity of Man-CDs-liposomes and their targeted recognition to HepG2 cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 11774048 , 11174047 , 11374046 ), and the Project from Key Laboratory for UV-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education (No. 130028723 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. The HRTEM image and particle size distributions of CDs are shown in the insets of (a). (b) AFM image of CDs. The inset is corresponding height profile of CDs. (c) XRD pattern of CDs. (d) Absorption spectrum (Abs) and fluorescence emission spectra (Em) of CDs under excitation with light of different wavelengths from 360 to 570 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) fluorescence quantum yield of CDs at different concentrations of H2O2. Excitation wavelength is 450 nm. (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) XPS patterns of CDs at different concentrations of H2O2 (0 mol/L, 0.12 mol/L and 9.82 mol/L).

(a) TEM images of liposomes; (b) CDs-liposomes; (c) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of CDs, liposomes and CDs-liposomes. (d) Fluorescence spectra of CDs and CDs-liposomes at excitation of 375 nm. (e) PL decay curves of CDs and CDs-liposomes at excitation of 366 nm, respectively.

Effect of (a) pH value and (b) concentration of NaCl on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs aqueous solution at 375 nm. (c) Fluorescence yield of CDs and CDs-liposomes for different times, respectively. Excitation wavelength is 375 nm. (d) Photographs of CDs (marked by squares on cuvette) and CDs-liposomse (marked by round on cuvette) in aqueous solutions for different times.

(a) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of liposomes, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes. TEM image (inset) of Man-CDs-liposomes after staining with phosphotungstic acid. (b) Zeta potential spectra of Man, CDs, liposomes, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes, respectively. (c) FT-IR spectra of D-mannose, CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes. (d) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes at excitation of 375 nm (solid line) and 510 nm (dotted line).
Cellular cytotoxicity of CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes, respectively.
CLSM fluorescence images of HepG2 cells after incubating with CDs (a), CDs-liposomes (b) and Man-CDs-liposomes (c) for 48 h under excitation wavelength of 375 and 532 nm. The scale bar is 10 µm.

Schematic illustration depicts the structure of Man-CDs-liposome. The process that Man-liposomes loading is displayed. The aldehyde groups of D-mannose can react with the amino on the hydrophilic head of liposomes to form imine by aldehyde-amide condensation reaction. Man-CDs-liposome can targeted recognition of HepG2 cells.

head.ce:title: Glycosylated liposomes loading carbon dots for targeted recognition to HepG2 cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Advanced Optoelectronic Functional Materials Research, Key Laboratory for UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of the Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Advanced Optoelectronic Functional Materials Research, Key Laboratory for UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of the Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Immunology, College of Basic Medical Sciences, Jilin University, 126 Xinmin Street, Changchun, Jilin Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Immunology, College of Basic Medical Sciences, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is important to obtain the targeted nanocarriers during cancer treatment. Herein, mannosylated liposomes encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) are provided for targeted recognition of liver cancer HepG2 cells, which is based on the specific interaction between D-mannose and glycoprotein on the surface of HepG2 cells. CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. Then, they were encapsulated into liposomes by hydrophobic force. The encapsulation of CDs into liposomes increases their stability and fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, D-mannose can also be inserted into liposomes by the aldehyde amine reaction between aldehyde groups of mannose molecules and amino groups of liposomes. The obtained D-mannose-CDs-liposomes (Man-CDs-liposomes) exhibit selectively tracking and efficiently labelling for cancer cells. The work highlights the potential application of CDs for bioimaging and diagnostic.

ce:section.ce:para: A main challenge for cancer research is how to recognize the cancer cell by targeted molecules on nanocarriers and achieve sensitive release at target sites [1–4]. The recognition of tumor cells at the molecular level is particularly important for cancer treatment. Different targeted recognition approaches have been developed by the interaction between biomarker molecules and cell surface receptor proteins including protein-protein interaction and protein-peptides interaction [5–8]. During the target recognition process, it is important to obtain the specific molecules for recognize cancer cell. Although the recognition of protein is very strong, it is difficult to obtain specific protein on cancer cell surface. As known, the carbohydrates have important biological functions such as specific molecular recognition by the interaction between carbohydrate and glycoprotein [9–12]. The cancer cells are covered with many glycans [13–16]. The glycans can express the corresponding carbohydrate-binding receptor glycoprotein. Different glycoproteins on the cancer cell surface can recognize specific saccharides molecules, which may forms the "fingerprint" of the cancer cell. So, glycosylated nanocarriers are thought to have great potential as cancer biomarkers [17–19]. Thus, it is feasible to achieve a targeted recognition by modifying the appropriate carbohydrate on the surface of drug carrier.

Liposomes have been widely used as carrier for delivering drugs in both vitro and vivo, which exhibits good biocompatibility and biodegradability. Liposomes are composed of phospholipids, cholesterols molecules to form the bilayer membrane structure including hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions [20–22]. Different drug and biomarker molecules can be loaded into the bilayer membrane of liposomes by hydrophilic-hydrophilic and hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction [23–25]. Glycosylated liposomes may be formed by incorporating carbohydrate molecules into liposomes. After bioactive materials are incorporated into liposomes, they can be carried to the specific cancer cells by the interaction of targeted recognition [26–28]. To trace the target recognition process, the fluorescence label for glycosylated liposomes may provide a chance to observe the interaction between carbohydrates and glycoprotein.

Carbon dots (CDs) are small carbon nanoparticles with different surface passivation including organic or polymeric molecules, which displays bright fluorescence emission in visible regions [29–31]. CDs have attracted more focus due to their excellent luminescent properties including bioimaging, drug delivery and biosensor. Compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs show good biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent photostability and versatile surface modification [32–34]. Krysmann et al. reported that the fluorescence of CDs originates from both the carbogenic core and surface states [35]. So, the fluorescent characteristics of CDs can be adjusted by changing their surface functional groups [36–38]. Based on the hydrophilic surface functional groups, CDs can be encapsulated into liposomes. The groups of liposomes can passivate the surface defect state of CDs, which increase their efficiency of fluorescence.

Herein, glycosylated liposomes encapsulating CDs are prepared for targeted recognition of cancer cells as shown in Scheme 1. The aldehyde groups of D-mannose can easily react with the amino groups of liposomes to form imine. It is well known that the expression protein of mannose receptor (MR) existed on the cell surface of HepG2 [16,39,40]. The mannose moiety on liposomes can recognize the HepG2 cell. Therefore, the modification of D-mannose on liposome surface can achieve the purpose of targeted recognition. Schematic illustration depicts the structure of Man-CDs-liposomes. The aldehyde groups of D-mannose can react with the amino on the hydrophilic head of liposomes in a weak alkaline environment to form imine by aldehyde-amide condensation reaction. CDs as fluorescence probe may track and image the targeted-recognition process. The CDs in liposomes have higher fluorescence quantum yields and stability than pure CDs because of their surface passivation by organic groups of liposomes. This provides a platform to targeted recognize cancer cell by the interaction between carbohydrate and glycoprotein.

Cholesterol was purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Soya lecithin and cephalin was obtained from Shanghai Tywei Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroform (CHCl3), p-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2), urea (CH4N2O), D-mannose, phosphotungstic acid (PTA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). 3-(4, 5-dimethy1-2-thiazoyl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals and reagents were used without further purification. Milli-Q water was used throughout the experiment.

The morphology of sample was characterized with a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips CM200 LaB6 microscope) operating at 200 kV and an atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon microscope, Scanasyst mode). And the lattice spacing in high-resolution TEM image was analyzed using Digital Micrograph 3.5. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Hitachi UH 4150 UV–visible-NIR spectrophotometer at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected on a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were obtained on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PTI-QuantaMaster 400), assembling with a Xenon lamp used for excitation. The zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were analyzed using a zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90) at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an ESCALAB250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher) by Al (kalpha) X-rays as radiation source (150 W and 15 kV). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a Rigaku D/max-2500 ×-ray diffractometer, using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The cell imaging was collected by a Nikon Inverted Microscope Eclipse Ti-U Main Body (Nikon, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with Nikon DS-U3 CCD, assembling with 375 and 532 nm laser devices used for excitation.

CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal conditions [37]. First, p-phenylenediamine (0.12 g), urea (0.12 g) and 30 mL of H2O were added into a reaction kettle (50 mL). Then the mixed solution was heated at 160 ℃ for 10.5 h. The obtained solution was purified via a 200 nm polycarbonate filter and dialyzing with membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) for 3 days, after cooling to room temperature. Finally, the CDs were stored at room temperature.

The lipid mixture of soya lecithin, cephalin and cholesterol at a molar ratio of 3:1:1 were dissolved in 4 mL of chloroform and dried under a rotary evaporator. Next, the dried lipid film was hydrated with 15 mL of CDs solution (75 μg/mL) and stirred at 42 ℃ for 3 h. The obtained solution was extruded through a 200 nm polycarbonate filter to form unilamellar liposomes. The unencapsulated CDs were removed via dialyzing with membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 12 kDa) for 3 days.

In order to prepare Man-CDs-liposome, an ethanol solution of D-mannose (0.01 g/mL) was dropped into the chloroform after adding the lipid mixture, followed by being dried under a rotary evaporator. To generate liposomes, the dried lipid thin layer was hydrated with an aqueous solution of CDs and stirred at 42 ℃ for 3 h, followed by being extruded through a 200 nm polycarbonate filter. The unencapsulated CDs were removed via dialyzing with membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 12 kDa) for 3 days. The obtained Man-CDs-liposome and CDs-liposome were stored at 4 ℃ in refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology characterization of CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes by negative staining
ce:section.ce:para: A drop (5 µL) of liposome solution were placed onto a 200-mesh formvar-coated copper grid and dried in air. CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes were treated by the same method. Then a drop (5 µL) of 1% phosphotungstic acid (PTA) was deposited on the coated-side for 2 min, respectively. The excess drop was removed by touching a filter paper. Finally, the grids were dried in air at 30 ℃.

Different concentrations of H2O2 (9.82, 4.91, 2.46, 1.23, 0.98, 0.49, 0.12, 0.06, 0.005 mol/L) were investigated to improve the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs. 1 mL of CDs solution (1.55 mg/mL) was added into 6 mL of H2O2. Then, 1 mL of CDs solution was added into 6 mL of H2O as a control experiment. The mixed solutions were treated by sonication for 1 h in an ice-water bath. Finally, the excess H2O2 was removed by dialyzing with membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) for 3 days.

The cytotoxicity of CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes for Hepa 1–6 cells in vitro were performed by the MTT assay. Hepa 1–6 cells were seeded into a 96-well plate for 24 h at a density of 1.0 × 105 cells/mL. The Hepa 1–6 cells were exposed to different concentration of CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes (0 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 75 μg/mL) for 48 h. Then the Hepa 1–6 cells were pulsed with 100 µL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) for 4 h. Next, 150 µL of dimethylsulphoxide (DMSO) was added into each well. And we measured the absorption of MTT at 492 nm. Cell viability was calculated by the optical density of each well. Each experiment was carried out three times, and the final results were averaged.

CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes were mixed with the cell culture medium, respectively. Then three kinds of mixtures were added into the HepG2 cells. The total medium was about 2 mL, respectively. Then, the HepG2 cells were incubated with CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes at the same concentration (75 μg/mL) for 48 h. And the temperature was 37 ℃ under 5% CO2. Finally, the HepG2 cells washed extensively (>3 times) with cold phosphate buffered solution (PBS) for cell imaging. The cell imaging was collected by a Nikon Inverted Microscope Ecllipse Ti-U Main body (Nikon, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with Nikon DS-U3 CCD. The images of CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes are collected in green channel (515–555 nm) under excitation of 375 nm and red channel (600–660 nm) under excitation of 532 nm.

The morphology and size distribution of the obtained CDs were characterized by TEM as shown in Fig. 1a. TEM image shows that the size of CDs is relatively uniform, exhibiting their size range is between 4 and 8 nm and the average size is 5.5 nm. The crystal lattice in high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in the insert of Fig. 1a displays the crystalline structure of CDs. Their interplanar distance is 0.21 nm, which is close to the (100) lattice spacing of graphite along the [100] direction. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image in Fig. 1b also indicated that the CDs are 6 ~ 8 nm. The results is similar to the size of CDs obtained from TEM. CDs shows a broad-angle XRD diffraction peak at about 25° corresponding to (002) in Fig. 1c, which further confirms their partial graphene structure and small size [41].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs show that a broad absorption peak exists in the visible region Fig. 1d. As reported, the absorption peaks of CDs at 277 nm and 299 nm correspond to the π–π*transitions of C˭C and C˭N bonds from carbon core, respectively. The absorption peak at 440 nm is assigned to the n–π* transition from the surface groups. The n–π* transition determines the fluorescence emission of CDs. The fluorescence of CDs shows excitation-dependent emission (Em) as in the case of most CDs. It can be seen from the Fig. 1d is that the fluorescence peak redshifts from 550 to 650 nm with the increase of the excitation wavelengths from 360 to 570 nm. The tunable emission behavior of CDs may be due to the surface functional groups and different band gaps from surface state [29,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence yield enhancement of CDs by oxidation treatment of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence efficiency of CDs is important for application. Herein, CDs are used for bio-detection. High photoluminescence efficiency for CDs is important for application. As mentioned, the fluorescence of CDs generally originates from the surface state. To enhance the fluorescence yields, the surface treatment of CDs is necessary. Herein, the oxidation treatment by H2O2 is used to change the surface groups so as to enhance fluorescence emission efficiency. To confirm the change of their fluorescence efficiency, fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is used to evaluate the photoluminescence properties. QY is the primary parameter characterizing emissive properties of fluorescence materials [42]. To calculate the QY, the appropriate excitation wavelength is important. The excitation wavelength of the quantum dots is determined by the excitation spectrum. For CDs, the excitation spectrum is provided in Supporting information (Fig. S1). The results indicate that the strongest excited peak is located near 450 nm for CDs with the treatment of 9.82 mol/L H2O2. Thus, the excited wavelength at 450 nm is chosen for comparing H2O2 quantum yield. The response of the fluorescence emission to H2O2 concentration ranging from 0.005 mol/L to 9.82 mol/L was evaluated. Fig. 2a shows the effect of oxidation of H2O2 on fluorescence emission. With an excitation of 450 nm, the fluorescence emission of CDs is gradually enhanced with increasing the amount of H2O2, indicating that the correlation between H2O2 concentration and fluorescence emission is concentration-dependent. Meanwhile, the fluorescence wavelength of CDs shows shift from 640 to 550 nm. To confirm the fluorescence efficiency of CDs, the corresponding QY is calculated according to the following Eq. (1) [42]:
(1)
ϕ
s
=
ϕ
r
×
(
A
r
/
A
s
)
×
(
S
s
/
S
r
)
×
(
n
s
2
/
n
r
2
)
… … … … … … … …
Where ϕ is QY, A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, S is the integral area under the fluorescence spectrum and n is the refractive index of solvent (1.33 for water and 1.36 for ethanol). The subscript s represents the sample, and r represents the reference (Rhodamine B in ethanol solution, ϕr = 0.68) [42]. Fig. 2b displays the effect of various H2O2 concentrations on the QY. The results indicate that the increase of H2O2 concentration enhance fluorescence emission efficiency. The QY of CDs is 1.43% without the treatment of H2O2. But, The QY of CDs reaches 25% with 9.82 mol/L H2O2. The reason for enhancement of quantum yield and the shift of fluorescence wavelength may be ascribed to the oxidation of CDs surface groups.

To confirm the change of surface functional groups by oxidation, the FT-IR spectra of CDs are provided before and after the treatment of H2O2 as shown in Fig. 2c. The different vibration bands at FT-IR spectroscopy come from different surface functional groups of CDs. Here, the CDs were prepared by hydrothermal reaction with p-phenylenediamine and urea. Several functional groups exist on the surface of CDs including hydroxyl, carboxylic, amino and amide groups. The CDs cores comprise C˭C and C˭N [36,37]. After treatment by H2O2, the functional groups on CDs may be oxidized. The FT-IR results show that low concentration H2O2 (0.12 mol/L) have little effect on the groups on CDs. However, high concentration H2O2 (9.82 mol/L) change the surface groups. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs displays that the obviously vibration peaks change. For pure CDs, the broad peak at ∼3678 cm−1 is corresponding to the O−H stretching vibrations. The peak at 3570 cm−1 is corresponding to N−H stretching vibrations of amino groups. The peaks at ∼1745, 1655 cm−1 are respectively assigned to C˭O stretching of carboxylic acid groups and C˭C vibration. The peaks at 1690 and 1518 cm−1 are corresponding to C˭N and N−H stretching vibrations of surface amide [36]. After the treatment of H2O2 with 9.82 mol/L, some surface functional groups should be oxidized, which leads to the change of FT-IR vibrations spectrum of CDs. The hydroxyl group on CDs may be oxidized to aldehyde group. Thus, the hydroxyl vibration absorption on CDs surface is decreased, but the absorption of C−H stretching vibration of aldehyde groups at 2850 cm−1 is increased. Otherwise, the vibration peak at 1386 cm−1 is ascribed to C−N of surface amino. The increase of vibration absorption of C−N may be due to the oxidation of C˭N on CDs by H2O2. The above results demonstrate that H2O2 induce more transformation of surface functional groups of CDs.

Furthermore, XPS spectra are characterized as shown in Fig. 2d to confirm the change of carbon and nitrogen on the surface of CDs. The contents ratios of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen is changed from 1:0.031:0.263 (pure CDs) to 1:0.794:0.694 with the treatment by concentration of H2O2 to 9.82 mol/L. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s are shown in Fig. S2. The results show that the C spectrum consists of four peaks at 284.6, 285.8, 286.4 and 288.4 eV, which is attributed to C˭C/C−C, C−N/C−O, C−O−C and COOH. The O spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.6 and 532.9 eV, representing C−O and C˭O. The N spectrum consist of two peaks at 398.7 and 399.1 eV, attributed C−NH2 and C−N−C. Comparing with pure CDs, the peak intensity of CDs at 288.4, 398.7 eV gradually increases with changing the concentration of H2O2 from 0.12 to 9.82 mol/L, which indicated that the content of nitrogen and carboxyl groups are increased. The results are corresponding to the change of FT-IR spectra of CDs after the treatment of H2O2·H2O2 exhibit strong oxidative properties. Before treatment of H2O2, the surface of CDs is mainly consist of hydroxyl groups. However, the treatment of H2O2 not only oxidize carbon−oxygen (C−O) bonds to form double carbon−oxygen (C˭O) bonds but also make the C˭N expose the surface of CDs. So, the mainly groups on CDs surface are carboxyl and amino groups after the oxidation of H2O2. The results correspond with the enhancement of CDs QY as the increase of oxygen and nitrogen contents. On the basis of the fluorescence properties and surface oxidation analysis, CDs by the treatment of H2O2 realize high fluorescence emission efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of liposomes loading CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Liposomes play an important role in transporting drug molecules and biological materials in vivo and in vitro. Phospholipids are the main components of liposomes including hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups. The bilayer structure of liposomes is formed based on the hydrophobic and hydrophobic interaction between different phospholipids molecules [22]. As mentioned above, the surface of CDs is covered with amino, carboxyl and C−O groups, which leads to the interaction between CDs and phospholipids. Then, CDs are encapsulated into liposomes by hydrophobic interaction. As shown in Fig. 3, the TEM images of pure liposomes (a) and liposomes loading CDs (b) are provided. The size of pure liposomes is about 80 nm. After loading CDs, their size has little change but the morphology of the liposomes has some bumps, which is thought from the encapsulation of CDs [43].

The absorption and fluorescence spectra are characterized as shown in Fig. 3c-d to confirm the effect of liposomes on the optical properties of CDs. CDs exhibit an absorption band at 470 nm. Pure liposomes have no absorption in visible region. After CDs were encapsulated into liposomes, the absorption peak of CDs at 470 nm is weakened, which is typical properties due to the encapsulation of CDs into liposome. Then, the fluorescence properties of CDs-liposomes were explored. As reported [44–46], the observed photoluminescence of CDs originate from surface state emission, which is due to hybridization of electronic states from carbon core and the functional groups. Herein, the C−O and amino groups may affect the fluorescence properties of CDs via electron transfer. It can be seen from Fig. 3d that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-liposome is significantly increased and their fluorescence emission shows an obvious blue shift comparing with CDs (blue shift is 18 nm, ex = 370 nm). It is known that the emission of CDs mainly from amino, carboxyl and C−O (n–π*). The amino groups in phospholipids may connect the surface detect of CDs to form more C−N bonds, which leads to the enhancement of the fluorescence emission. Meanwhile, the blue shift from fluorescence emission peaks of CDs-liposomes comparing to pure CDs may be due to the change of emission center from carboxyl to amino groups. The fluorescence decay of CDs and CDs-liposomes in solution is investigated as shown Fig. 3e. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs is about 2.04 ns, but the average lifetime of CDs-liposomes is about 1.51 ns. The fluorescence lifetime is mainly represented by the fluorescence decay to 1⁄e of maximum intensity. The decrease of fluorescence lifetime after encapsulating CDs is due to the electrons are partially taken away by C−O in the process of electronic transition. These results show that fluorescence properties of CDs are obviously improved by encapsulation of liposomes, which is important for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of liposomes loading with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the biological application, the fluorescent stability of CDs in liposomes is evaluated as shown in Fig. 4. Firstly, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was compared from 3 to 11 in Fig. 4a. The results show that fluorescence intensity of CDs in liposomes is almost unchanged with increasing pH. Then, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs is investigated as shown in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity of CDs is almost unaffected, even if the concentration of NaCl reaches 2 mol/L.

Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield decay curves of pure CDs and CDs-liposome are compared at the same intervals, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4c, the QY of pure CDs show obviously decrease with time. However, the QYs of CDs encapsulated in liposomes show slowly decrease at two months. The photographs in Fig. 4d of pure CDs and CDs-liposomes solutions are taken at different times. After two months, the solution color of the CDs is changed from reddish brown to yellowish brown. However, the solution color of CDs-liposomes show still light reddish brown after two month. The results indicate that liposomes can not only improve the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs, but also can improve stability of CDs.

CDs in liposomes show excellent fluorescence QY and stability. However, the specific recognition to target cell is important for applying CDs-liposomes into bio-recognition. As known, the surface of cancer cell has many glycoprotein molecules. The interaction between sugar and glycoprotein is of high selectivity. For example, D-mannose may recognize the mannose receptor of liver cancer cell. So, D-mannose molecules are designed to insert into liposomes loading CDs by aldehyde-amide condensation reaction. That is the reactions between the aldehyde groups in mannose and the amino groups of phospholipids.

Firstly, the absorption spectra in Fig. 5a show that CDs are successfully encapsulated in liposomes due to a broad peak appears in visible range after encapsulating CDs. Furthermore, no change in the absorption spectrum of Man-CDs-liposomes is observed after modifying D-mannose molecules. The size and morphology of the CDs-liposomes modified by D-mannose (inset) have little change comparing with CDs-liposomes, which is about 80 nm. The modification of D-mannose molecules on CDs-liposomes may affect the surface potential. So, the zeta potential values of D-mannose, CDs, liposome, CDs-liposome and Man-CDs-liposome were measured. The results show that the zeta potential of the CDs and liposomes are respectively –3.62 and −12.63 mV in Fig. 5b. The zeta potential of CDs-liposomes is −14.03 mV, which also demonstrates the successful preparation of CDs-liposome. The zeta potential of the Man-CDs-liposome is changed to −15.37 mV after modifying the D-mannose on the surface of CDs-liposome. The surface potential demonstrates that D-mannose molecules may exist on the surface of liposome.

To confirm the existence of D-mannose, FT-IR spectra of D-mannose, CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes are provided as shown in Fig. 5c. D-Mannose molecules exhibit a peak at 3295 cm−1 corresponding to vibration of O−H. The peaks at 1741 cm−1 is attributed to the C˭O vibration of aldehyde groups in mannose molecules. The FT-IR spectra of pure CDs and CDs-liposomes are all exhibit the surface group's vibration including carboxyl, amino groups. However, Man-CDs-liposomes appear a broad peak from 2917 cm−1 to 3601 cm−1 comparing with D-mannose molecules, which includes the vibration of O−H, N−H, respectively. The vibration at 1697 cm−1 is from C˭N of imine. It is known that the aldehyde groups in mannose molecules can react easily with the amino groups of phospholipids to form an imine by aldehyde-amide condensation reaction. So, the FT-IR spectrum of Man-CDs-liposomes confirms the modification of the D-mannose on the liposomes surface.

To observe the effect of modification of D-mannose on fluorescence emission of CDs-liposomes, three samples including pure CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes are respectively as excited at 375 and 510 nm as shown in Fig. 5d. The solid lines represent three samples excited by 375 nm. The dotted lines represent three samples excited by 510 nm. The results indicated that different excited wavelength leads to different fluorescence emission of CDs at 550 and 650 nm. After encapsulating into liposomes, the emission spectra of CDs-liposome show obviously blue-shift for about 30 nm due to the interaction between surface functional groups and liposomes. Man-CDs-liposomes are observed the fluorescence emission similar to CDs-liposomes. Furthermore, the enhancement in fluorescence intensity of CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes were observed comparing with that of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cytotoxicity of Man-CDs-liposomes and their targeted recognition to HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To apply in bio-recognition, the cytotoxicity of CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes are tested by the standard MTT assay using Hepa 1–6 cells as shown in Fig. 6. The Hepa 1–6 cells are respectively incubated with CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes at concentrations from 0 to 75 μg/L for 48 h. The results show that the viability of Hepa 1–6 cells are all over 87% even at high concentration, indicating they have low cytotoxicity and have excellent biocompatibility. So, Man-CDs-liposomes can be used for targeted applications.

As known, D-mannose may recognize the glycoprotein on liver cancer cell (HepG2 cells). So, the application of Man-CDs-liposomes for imaging the targeted recognition to HepG2 cells was investigated by confocal fluorescent microscope (CLSM). Fig. 7 displays the typical images of HepG2 cells after incubation with CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes. The nucleus are dyed by 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI). The bright blue fluorescence from CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes can easily be seen as shown in Fig. 7a-c. The green and red emission in HepG2 cells are from CDs due to different excited wavelength including 375 and 532 nm. It is noted that the fluorescence emission of CDs depends on the excited wavelength. The overlays with the corresponding dark-field images show the distribution of CDs, CDs-liposomes and Man-CDs-liposomes intracellular and extracellular. Firstly, CDs show obviously endocytic uptake due to their small size effect, but that is nonspecific interaction with cell. Then, the fluorescence images of CDs-liposomes (Fig. 7b) are observed after incubating with HepG2 cells. The images show that the emission of CDs is weak and mainly located over the cell membrane. No fluorescence signals are observed at the location of the nucleus, which is due to that large amount of CDs-liposomes cannot be internalized into the cells. Instead, most CDs-liposomes are absorbed on the cell membrane by electrostatic interaction between CDs-liposomes and the cells. On the contrary, the fluorescence of Man-CDs-liposomes (Fig. 7c) is located over the cell membrane and cytoplasm. And the location of the nucleus has weak fluorescence, which is due to the CDs in the cytoplasm. This indicates that a large amount of Man-CDs-liposomes are internalized into the HepG2 cells via mannose receptor-mediated endocytosis. Comparing with CDs-liposomes, HepG2 cells show stronger fluorescence when incubating with Man-CDs-liposomes. The higher uptake of Man-CDs-liposomes in HepG2 cells is based on the expression of mannose receptors on the surface of HepG2 cells. Man-CDs-liposomes can more easily bind to the surface of HepG2 cells by the targeted recognition to mannose receptors, which can easily promote the particle uptake of cancer cells as compared with electrostatic interaction of CDs-liposomes. Such an obviously difference in the cellular uptake suggests that Man-CDs-liposomes exhibit excellent targeted recognition. Thus, Man-CDs-liposomes realized the targeted recognition and fluorescence image to liver cancer HepG2 cells simultaneously, which is important to apply Man-CDs-liposomes for drug delivery and treatment.

In summary, glycosylated liposomes loading CDs have been fabricated, which exhibits bifunctional properties including targeted recognition and imaging to HepG 2 cells. The high fluorescence emission of CDs is firstly important to image the targeted recognition process as probe. Herein, the fluorescence QY is enhanced from 1.43% to 22.8% by the treatment of H2O2. The mechanism of QY enhancement is discussed due to the increase of nitrogen and oxygen content on surface states after oxidation reaction. Based on the specific recognition between D-mannose and glycoprotein on liver cancer HepG 2 cells, D-mannose molecules are linked with liposomes by aldehyde-amide condensation reaction. The cell viability assay shows that Man-CDs-liposomes have low cytotoxicity and are suitable for biological application. The application of Man-CDs-liposomes for imaging the targeted recognition to HepG2 cells was investigated by CLSM. The results show that Man-CDs-liposomes can not only use the specific recognition, but also can be used for tracking the interaction between carbohydrates and glycoprotein by the fluorescence image of CDs. That provides a platform for multifunctional application of Man-CDs-liposomes in biological recognition and image.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11774048, 11174047, 11374046), and the Project from Key Laboratory for UV-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education (No. 130028723).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.01.069.
